Maths - Number and operations.skill: Classify rational numbers using a diagram
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers classifying rational numbers for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.2(A)). Students should understand how to organize numbers into categories using diagrams. Key concepts: rational numbers include all numbers that can be expressed as a fraction a/b where b is not 0; natural numbers (1, 2, 3...) are a subset of whole numbers (0, 1, 2, 3...); whole numbers are a subset of integers (...-2, -1, 0, 1, 2...); integers are a subset of rational numbers; Venn diagrams show these nested relationships. Vocabulary: rational number, integer, whole number, natural number, classify, subset, Venn diagram, fraction, decimal, positive, negative, set. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers classifying numbers into categories for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.2(A)). Students should understand the relationships among different number sets. Key concepts: natural numbers (counting numbers: 1, 2, 3...) are a subset of whole numbers (0, 1, 2, 3...); whole numbers are a subset of integers (...-2, -1, 0, 1, 2...); integers are a subset of rational numbers (any number expressible as a/b where b is not 0); students should classify given numbers into the correct categories and explain why. Vocabulary: classify, natural number, whole number, integer, rational number, subset, category, set, fraction, decimal, positive, negative. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers understanding opposite integers for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.2(B)). Students should understand that every integer has an opposite that is the same distance from zero on the number line but on the opposite side. Key concepts: the opposite of a positive number is negative, and vice versa; the opposite of 0 is 0; opposites add up to 0 (additive inverse); on a number line, opposites are equidistant from zero on opposite sides; the notation for the opposite of n is -n. Vocabulary: opposite, integer, additive inverse, negative, positive, zero, number line, distance, equidistant, pair, sign. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers absolute value for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.2(B)). Students should understand that absolute value represents the distance of a number from zero on the number line. Key concepts: absolute value is always non-negative; the absolute value of both 5 and -5 is 5; notation uses vertical bars: |n|; absolute value represents magnitude without direction; it is useful for real-world contexts like distance, temperature change, and debt. Students should evaluate absolute value expressions and compare values using absolute value. Vocabulary: absolute value, distance, zero, number line, magnitude, positive, non-negative, notation, bars, opposite, integer, compare. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Students should choose appropriate scales with fractional values and accurately place data points. This topic covers creating frequency charts for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(A)). Students should understand how to organize data into a frequency chart (frequency table) that shows how often each value or category occurs. Key concepts: frequency is the number of times a value appears in a data set; data is organized into categories or intervals; tally marks can be used to count occurrences; the frequency column shows the total count for each category. Students should accurately count and record data, choose appropriate categories or intervals, and verify that the total of all frequencies equals the number of data points. Vocabulary: frequency, chart, table, tally, category, interval, data, count, total, organize, value. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers creating histograms for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(A)). Students should understand how to display continuous numerical data grouped into intervals using connected bars. Key concepts: histograms group data into equal-width intervals (bins); the height of each bar shows the frequency of data in that interval; bars are connected (no gaps) because the data is continuous; the horizontal axis shows the intervals and the vertical axis shows frequency; choosing appropriate interval sizes affects how the data distribution appears. Vocabulary: histogram, interval, bin, frequency, continuous data, axis, scale, distribution, bar, gap, range, width. This topic covers creating stem-and-leaf plots for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(A)). Students should understand how to organize numerical data into a stem-and-leaf plot that shows the shape and distribution of data. Key concepts: the stem represents the leading digit(s) (usually tens place) and the leaf represents the trailing digit (usually ones place); leaves must be ordered from least to greatest; a key explains how to read the values; stem-and-leaf plots preserve individual data values while showing the overall distribution. Students should practice organizing data sets into stem-and-leaf plots accurately. Vocabulary: stem, leaf, plot, data, key, digit, tens place, ones place, distribution, order, organize, leading digit. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers box plots for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(A)). Students should understand how to create and interpret box plots (box-and-whisker plots) that display the five-number summary of a data set. Students should create box plots from data sets and use them to compare distributions. Vocabulary: box plot, minimum, maximum, median, quartile, Q1, Q3, interquartile range, IQR, whisker, five-number summary, spread, center. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Key concepts: mean is the sum of all values divided by the number of values (average); median is the middle value when data is ordered (or average of two middle values); mode is the most frequently occurring value; range is the difference between the maximum and minimum values. Students should understand when each measure is most useful and how outliers affect mean versus median. Vocabulary: mean, median, mode, range, average, center, spread, data set, outlier, sum, order, frequency, calculate, interpret. Problems should involve computing and interpreting these measures from data sets and graphs appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers describing distributions in data displays for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(B)). Students should understand how to describe the overall shape, center, spread, and unusual features of data distributions. Key concepts: shape can be symmetric (balanced), skewed left (tail extends left), or skewed right (tail extends right); center is described using mean or median; spread is described using range or IQR; students should identify clusters (groups of data), gaps (empty intervals), and peaks; a thorough description mentions shape, center, spread, and any outliers. Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Calculate range, quartiles, and interquartile range
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers calculating range, quartiles, and interquartile range for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(C)). Students should understand measures of spread that describe how data is distributed. Key concepts: range = maximum - minimum; the first quartile (Q1) is the median of the lower half of data; the third quartile (Q3) is the median of the upper half; interquartile range (IQR) = Q3 - Q1, representing the spread of the middle 50% of data; IQR is less affected by outliers than range. Students should calculate these measures from ordered data sets. Vocabulary: range, quartile, Q1, Q3, interquartile range, IQR, median, minimum, maximum, spread, distribution, lower half, upper half, outlier. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers identifying outliers for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(B)). Students should understand that outliers are data values that are significantly different from the rest of the data. Key concepts: an outlier is a value that is much higher or much lower than most other values in the data set; outliers can be identified visually on graphs or mathematically using the IQR (values below Q1 - 1.5*IQR or above Q3 + 1.5*IQR); outliers significantly affect the mean but have less impact on the median; understanding outliers helps evaluate which measure of center best represents the data. Vocabulary: outlier, data set, mean, median, range, interquartile range, IQR, extreme value, affect, skew, distribution. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Vocabulary: relative frequency, frequency, total, fraction, decimal, percentage, proportion, category, data, table, compare, ratio. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers creating bar graphs for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(D)). Students should understand how to display categorical data using bars of varying heights or lengths. Key concepts: bar graphs use rectangular bars to represent data for different categories; the height or length of each bar corresponds to the frequency or value; bars should be equal in width with equal spacing; axes must be labeled with the category names and numerical scale; a title describes the data being shown. Students should choose appropriate scales and accurately draw bars. Vocabulary: bar graph, category, axis, scale, title, label, frequency, horizontal, vertical, data, height, interval. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting circle graphs for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). Students should understand how to read and analyze circle graphs (pie charts) that show parts of a whole. Key concepts: each section of the circle represents a category as a fraction, decimal, or percentage of the whole; all sections must add up to 100% or the whole; larger sections represent greater proportions; students should compare sections and calculate actual values when given totals. Vocabulary: circle graph, pie chart, section, category, percentage, fraction, whole, proportion, compare, total, part, represent. Problems should involve reading circle graphs and calculating values appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting line plots for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). Students should understand how to read and analyze data displayed in line plots to answer questions and draw conclusions. Key concepts: identify the most and least common values (mode); find the range (difference between greatest and least values); determine the total number of data points; calculate the sum of all values using the plot; answer comparison questions (how many more/fewer). Students should use operations with the data values, including fractions when applicable, to solve problems. Vocabulary: line plot, data, frequency, mode, range, total, interpret, analyze, compare, most common, least common, sum, difference. Problems should involve drawing conclusions from line plots appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Maths - Measurement and data.skill: Create and interpret line plots with fractions
Maths - Measurement and data.prompt: This topic covers creating line plots with fractions for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). Students should understand how to organize fractional data on a number line using X marks or dots to create a line plot. Key concepts: line plots display data along a number line; each data point is represented by a mark above the corresponding value; when data involves fractions, the number line must include fractional increments (halves, quarters, eighths); data should be sorted and counted before plotting. Students should choose appropriate scales with fractional values and accurately place data points. Vocabulary: line plot, data, fraction, number line, frequency, scale, increment, mark, value, half, quarter, eighth. Common errors include using incorrect scales, miscounting data points, or placing marks at wrong positions. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting histograms for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). Students should understand how to read and analyze histograms to describe data distributions and answer questions. Key concepts: identifying which intervals have the most and fewest data points; determining the total number of data points; describing the shape of the distribution (symmetric, skewed left, skewed right); identifying peaks, gaps, and clusters; comparing data across intervals. Vocabulary: histogram, interval, frequency, distribution, symmetric, skewed, peak, gap, cluster, range, interpret, analyze. Problems should involve reading histograms and answering questions about data distributions appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting stem-and-leaf plots for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). Students should understand how to read and analyze data from stem-and-leaf plots to answer questions. Key concepts: reading individual values by combining the stem and leaf; finding the minimum and maximum values; determining the range, median, and mode from the plot; counting the total number of data points; identifying clusters, gaps, and outliers in the distribution. Vocabulary: stem-and-leaf plot, stem, leaf, key, median, mode, range, minimum, maximum, cluster, gap, outlier, distribution, interpret, analyze. Students should practice extracting information and making comparisons from stem-and-leaf plots. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers box plots for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). Students should understand how to create and interpret box plots (box-and-whisker plots) that display the five-number summary of a data set. Key concepts: the five-number summary consists of minimum, first quartile (Q1), median (Q2), third quartile (Q3), and maximum; the box spans from Q1 to Q3 representing the interquartile range (IQR); the line inside the box marks the median; whiskers extend from the box to the minimum and maximum; box plots show the spread and center of data. Students should create box plots from data sets and use them to compare distributions. Vocabulary: box plot, minimum, maximum, median, quartile, Q1, Q3, interquartile range, IQR, whisker, five-number summary, spread, center. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers identifying statistical questions for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(B)). Students should understand the difference between statistical questions (those that anticipate variability in the data) and non-statistical questions (those with a single, definite answer). Key concepts: a statistical question is one that can be answered by collecting data that vary (e.g., 'How many hours do sixth graders sleep each night?'); a non-statistical question has one specific answer (e.g., 'How many hours did I sleep last night?'); statistical questions require data collection, organization, and analysis. Vocabulary: statistical question, non-statistical question, data, variability, survey, collect, analyze, sample, population, variable. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers comparing checking accounts for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(A)). Students should understand what checking accounts are and how to compare different account options. Key concepts: a checking account is a bank account used for everyday transactions like paying bills and making purchases; different accounts may have different features including monthly fees, minimum balance requirements, ATM access, online banking, and overdraft protection; some accounts earn interest while others do not; students should evaluate which account best fits a person's needs based on their financial situation. Vocabulary: checking account, monthly fee, minimum balance, overdraft, ATM, debit card, direct deposit, online banking, transaction, compare, evaluate, financial institution. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers debit cards and credit cards for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(B)). Students should understand how debit and credit cards work and the key differences between them. Key concepts: debit cards withdraw money directly from a linked bank account; credit cards borrow money from a credit card company that must be repaid; credit cards charge interest on unpaid balances; responsible credit card use involves paying the full balance each month; debit cards help control spending since you can only spend what you have; credit cards can build credit history but also lead to debt if misused. Vocabulary: debit card, credit card, interest, balance, minimum payment, credit limit, annual fee, PIN, transaction, debt, credit history, billing cycle. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers check registers for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(C)). Students should understand how to use a check register to track deposits, withdrawals, and account balances. Key concepts: a check register is a record of all transactions in a checking account; each entry includes the date, description, amount, and running balance; deposits increase the balance while withdrawals, checks, and debit card purchases decrease it; the running balance shows how much money is in the account at any given time; keeping an accurate register helps avoid overdrawing the account. Vocabulary: check register, deposit, withdrawal, balance, transaction, date, description, amount, running balance, overdraft, reconcile, debit, credit. Students should practice recording transactions and calculating running balances. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers credit reports for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(D)). Students should understand what credit reports are and why they matter for financial health. Key concepts: a credit report is a record of a person's borrowing and repayment history; it includes information about loans, credit cards, and payment history; lenders use credit reports to decide whether to approve loans and at what interest rate; a good credit history (paying bills on time, keeping balances low) leads to better borrowing terms; a poor credit history can make it harder and more expensive to borrow money. Vocabulary: credit report, credit score, credit history, lender, borrower, loan, interest rate, payment history, debt, responsible, identity, financial health. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers credit reports for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(E)). Students should understand what credit reports are and why they matter for financial health. Key concepts: a credit report is a record of a person's borrowing and repayment history; it includes information about loans, credit cards, and payment history; lenders use credit reports to decide whether to approve loans and at what interest rate; a good credit history (paying bills on time, keeping balances low) leads to better borrowing terms; a poor credit history can make it harder and more expensive to borrow money. Vocabulary: credit report, credit score, credit history, lender, borrower, loan, interest rate, payment history, debt, responsible, identity, financial health. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers credit reports for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(F)). Students should understand what credit reports are and why they matter for financial health. Key concepts: a credit report is a record of a person's borrowing and repayment history; it includes information about loans, credit cards, and payment history; lenders use credit reports to decide whether to approve loans and at what interest rate; a good credit history (paying bills on time, keeping balances low) leads to better borrowing terms; a poor credit history can make it harder and more expensive to borrow money. Vocabulary: credit report, credit score, credit history, lender, borrower, loan, interest rate, payment history, debt, responsible, identity, financial health. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers paying for college for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(G)). Students should understand the various ways to fund higher education and the importance of planning ahead. Key concepts: college costs include tuition, fees, room and board, books, and supplies; funding sources include savings, scholarships (merit-based and need-based), grants (usually need-based and do not need to be repaid), student loans (must be repaid with interest), and work-study programs; starting to save early makes college more affordable; understanding the difference between types of financial aid helps students make informed decisions about their education. Vocabulary: tuition, scholarship, grant, student loan, financial aid, savings, interest, room and board, FAFSA, merit-based, need-based, work-study. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers occupations, education, and income for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.14(H)). Students should understand the relationship between education, career choices, and earning potential. Key concepts: different occupations require different levels of education and training; generally, higher levels of education lead to higher earning potential; career options include jobs requiring a high school diploma, technical certifications, associate degrees, bachelor's degrees, and advanced degrees; income varies by occupation, location, experience, and education level. Students should explore various career paths and understand the investment of time and money in education relative to potential earnings. Vocabulary: occupation, career, education, income, salary, wages, degree, certification, training, earning potential, job market, skills. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Maths - Miscellaneous.prompt: This topic covers ratios and rates for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.4(B)). Students should understand how ratios compare two quantities and rates compare two quantities with different units. Key concepts: a ratio compares two quantities (e.g., 3 boys to 5 girls = 3:5); a rate compares quantities with different units (e.g., 60 miles per 2 hours); a unit rate has a denominator of 1 (e.g., 30 miles per hour); ratios can be expressed as fractions, with colons, or with the word 'to'; equivalent ratios represent the same relationship. Vocabulary: ratio, rate, unit rate, equivalent, compare, quantity, proportion, per, fraction, colon, simplify, relationship. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers unit rates and percentages for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.4(D)). Students should understand how to calculate unit rates and work with percentages fluently. Key concepts: a unit rate expresses a quantity per one unit (divide to find it); unit rates are useful for comparison (best buy, fastest speed); percent means per hundred; converting between fractions, decimals, and percents; finding a percent of a number by multiplying; percentages are used in sales tax, tips, discounts, and interest. Vocabulary: unit rate, percent, per, rate, fraction, decimal, convert, calculate, compare, proportion, hundred, percentage point. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers dividing fractions by fractions for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.3(E)). Students should understand how to divide any fraction by another fraction using the reciprocal. Key concepts: dividing by a fraction is equivalent to multiplying by its reciprocal (a/b divided by c/d = a/b x d/c); the reciprocal of c/d is d/c; convert mixed numbers to improper fractions before dividing; interpret division of fractions in context (how many groups of the divisor fit into the dividend); simplify results when possible. Vocabulary: divide, fraction, reciprocal, invert, multiply, mixed number, improper fraction, simplify, quotient, divisor, dividend, context. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers operations with multi-digit decimals for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.3(E)). Students should understand how to add, subtract, multiply, and divide decimal numbers fluently. Key concepts: for addition and subtraction, align decimal points and compute as with whole numbers; for multiplication, multiply as whole numbers then count total decimal places for the product; for division, adjust the divisor to a whole number by moving decimal points equally; estimate to verify reasonableness for all operations. Vocabulary: decimal, add, subtract, multiply, divide, decimal point, align, estimate, place value, tenths, hundredths, thousandths, operation, algorithm. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Maths - Miscellaneous.skill: Greatest common factor and least common multiple
Maths - Miscellaneous.prompt: This topic covers factors and multiples for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.7(A)). Students should understand the relationship between factors, multiples, and divisibility. Key concepts: a factor of a number divides it evenly (no remainder); a multiple of a number is the product of that number and any whole number; factor pairs multiply to give the original number; the GCF (greatest common factor) is the largest factor shared by two numbers; the LCM (least common multiple) is the smallest multiple shared by two numbers; divisibility rules help identify factors quickly. Vocabulary: factor, multiple, divisible, GCF, LCM, factor pair, product, remainder, divisibility rule, prime, composite. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers the distributive property for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.7(D)). Students should understand how to apply the distributive property to expand and factor expressions. Key concepts: a(b + c) = ab + ac; the property works with both numerical and algebraic expressions; area models visually demonstrate distribution; factoring is the reverse process (finding the GCF and writing as a product); the distributive property simplifies mental math and helps generate equivalent expressions. Vocabulary: distributive property, expand, factor, expression, GCF, product, sum, area model, equivalent, simplify, coefficient, term, algebraic. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers introduction to integers and negative numbers for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.2(B)). Students should understand that negative numbers extend the number line below zero and are used in many real-world contexts. Key concepts: integers include positive numbers, negative numbers, and zero; negative numbers are less than zero; on a number line, negative numbers are to the left of zero; real-world contexts include temperature below zero, below sea level, debt, and loss; opposite integers are equidistant from zero on opposite sides. Vocabulary: integer, positive, negative, zero, opposite, number line, below zero, debt, sea level, temperature, sign, distance. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers absolute value for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.2(B)). Students should understand that absolute value represents the distance of a number from zero on the number line. Key concepts: absolute value is always non-negative; the absolute value of both 5 and -5 is 5; notation uses vertical bars: |n|; absolute value represents magnitude without direction; it is useful for real-world contexts like distance, temperature change, and debt. Students should evaluate absolute value expressions and compare values using absolute value. Vocabulary: absolute value, distance, zero, number line, magnitude, positive, non-negative, notation, bars, opposite, integer, compare. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers the coordinate plane for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.11(A)). Students should understand how to use the coordinate system with all four quadrants. Key concepts: the coordinate plane has a horizontal x-axis and vertical y-axis intersecting at the origin (0,0); four quadrants are numbered I through IV counterclockwise from the upper right; points are identified by ordered pairs (x, y); the x-coordinate indicates horizontal position and the y-coordinate indicates vertical position; points can have positive or negative coordinates. Vocabulary: coordinate plane, x-axis, y-axis, origin, quadrant, ordered pair, positive, negative, plot, graph, horizontal, vertical. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Maths - Miscellaneous.skill: Writing and evaluating numerical expressions with exponents
Maths - Miscellaneous.prompt: This topic covers exponents for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.7(A)). Students should understand that an exponent indicates repeated multiplication of a base number. Key concepts: in b^n, b is the base and n is the exponent; the exponent tells how many times to multiply the base by itself; any number to the power of 1 equals itself; any nonzero number to the power of 0 equals 1; students should evaluate expressions with exponents and write repeated multiplication using exponential notation. Vocabulary: exponent, base, power, squared, cubed, repeated multiplication, evaluate, expression, notation, exponential. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers writing algebraic expressions from verbal descriptions for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.7(B)). Students should translate real-world situations and word phrases into algebraic expressions using variables. Key concepts: assign variables to unknown quantities; identify operations from context words (added to, less than, times, divided by, product of); coefficients multiply variables; constants are fixed values; the order of terms matters for subtraction and division. Vocabulary: algebraic expression, variable, coefficient, constant, term, translate, verbal, operation, add, subtract, multiply, divide, represent, write. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers evaluating algebraic expressions by substituting values for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.7(A)). Students should understand how to replace variables with given values and compute the result. Key concepts: substitute the given value for each variable in the expression; follow the order of operations to evaluate; expressions with exponents require computing powers before multiplication; expressions may contain multiple variables each with their own value; evaluate step by step showing work. Vocabulary: evaluate, algebraic expression, substitute, variable, value, order of operations, exponent, compute, simplify, replace, result. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers solving one-variable equations and inequalities for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.10(A)). Students should understand how to isolate a variable using inverse operations. Key concepts: use inverse operations (addition/subtraction, multiplication/division) to isolate the variable; maintain balance by performing the same operation on both sides; equations have one solution while inequalities have many; when multiplying or dividing an inequality by a negative number, reverse the inequality sign; graph solutions on number lines. Vocabulary: equation, inequality, variable, solve, inverse operation, isolate, balance, solution, graph, number line, one-step, verify. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers dependent and independent variables for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.6(A)). Students should understand how to identify which variable depends on the other in a relationship. Key concepts: the independent variable is what is changed or controlled (input); the dependent variable responds to or depends on the independent variable (output); in y = 2x + 3, x is independent and y is dependent; in tables, the independent variable is usually in the left column; in graphs, it goes on the x-axis. Vocabulary: independent variable, dependent variable, input, output, relationship, cause, effect, table, graph, x-axis, y-axis, equation, change. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers calculating the area of triangles and quadrilaterals for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.8(D)). Students should apply area formulas for various shapes. Key concepts: area of a rectangle = length x width; area of a parallelogram = base x height; area of a triangle = 1/2 x base x height; area of a trapezoid = 1/2 x (base1 + base2) x height; the height is always perpendicular to the base; composite figures can be decomposed into these simpler shapes. Vocabulary: area, triangle, quadrilateral, rectangle, parallelogram, trapezoid, base, height, perpendicular, formula, square units, decompose. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers volume of right rectangular prisms for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.8(D)). Students should understand how to calculate the volume of rectangular boxes using the formula and by reasoning about layers. Key concepts: volume = length x width x height (V = lwh); volume can also be computed as base area x height (V = Bh); volume is measured in cubic units; packing unit cubes into a prism demonstrates the formula; students should solve both computation and word problems involving volume. Vocabulary: volume, rectangular prism, length, width, height, cubic units, formula, base area, three-dimensional, capacity, space, layer, unit cube. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers surface area using nets for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.8(B)). Students should understand that surface area is the total area of all faces of a 3D figure. Key concepts: a net is a 2D pattern that folds into a 3D shape; the surface area equals the sum of the areas of all faces; for rectangular prisms, there are three pairs of congruent rectangular faces; for cubes, all six faces are congruent squares; students should unfold figures into nets and calculate each face's area. Vocabulary: surface area, net, face, edge, rectangular prism, cube, congruent, unfold, fold, square units, three-dimensional, calculate. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers statistical variability for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(B)). Students should understand that data naturally varies and that statistical measures help describe this variability. Key concepts: variability describes how spread out data values are; measures of center (mean, median) describe the typical value; measures of spread (range, IQR, MAD) describe how much values differ; data distributions can be described by their shape, center, and spread; understanding variability helps make informed conclusions from data. Vocabulary: variability, spread, center, distribution, range, IQR, mean absolute deviation, mean, median, data, describe, compare, typical, variation. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers measures of center for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(C)). Students should understand how mean, median, and mode each describe the center of a data set differently. Key concepts: mean is the sum of all values divided by the count (sensitive to outliers); median is the middle value when data is ordered (resistant to outliers); mode is the most frequent value (may not exist or may be multiple); each measure has advantages depending on the data distribution; understanding when to use each measure is important for data analysis. Vocabulary: mean, median, mode, average, center, data set, outlier, sum, count, order, frequency, measure of center, distribution, resistant. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers data displays including histograms and box plots for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(A)). Students should understand how to create and interpret various data displays to analyze distributions. Key concepts: histograms display frequency of data within intervals; box plots show the five-number summary (minimum, Q1, median, Q3, maximum); different displays highlight different aspects of the data; choosing the right display depends on the question being asked. Vocabulary: histogram, box plot, frequency, interval, five-number summary, median, quartile, distribution, display, create, interpret, compare, analyze. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers summarizing data distributions for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(B)). Students should understand how to describe data sets using measures of center, spread, and shape. Key concepts: a complete summary includes the shape of the distribution (symmetric, skewed), a measure of center (mean or median), and a measure of spread (range, IQR); for symmetric distributions, mean and median are close; for skewed distributions, median is more representative; outliers affect mean but not median significantly. Vocabulary: summarize, distribution, shape, center, spread, symmetric, skewed, mean, median, range, IQR, outlier, describe, representative. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

This topic covers proportional relationships for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.5(A)). Students should understand that two quantities are in a proportional relationship when their ratio is constant. Key concepts: in a proportional relationship, y = kx where k is the constant of proportionality (unit rate); tables show a constant ratio between corresponding values; graphs of proportional relationships are straight lines through the origin; equivalent ratios form proportional relationships. Vocabulary: proportional, relationship, constant of proportionality, unit rate, ratio, equivalent, graph, table, equation, origin, straight line, scale factor. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe cell theory development and explain organisms are composed of cells
Science.prompt: This topic covers cells and cell theory for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). The TEKS standard states: Describe cell theory development and explain organisms are composed of cells. Students should understand that cells are the basic units of life and that all living organisms are composed of one or more cells. Key concepts: cell theory states that all living things are made of cells, cells are the basic unit of structure and function, and all cells come from pre-existing cells; plant cells have cell walls, chloroplasts, and large vacuoles that animal cells lack; both plant and animal cells have a cell membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, and mitochondria; organisms can be unicellular or multicellular. Vocabulary: cell, cell theory, cell membrane, cell wall, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria, chloroplast, vacuole, organelle, unicellular, multicellular, prokaryotic, eukaryotic. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe cell theory development and explain that organisms are composed of one or more cells
Science.prompt: This topic covers cells and cell theory for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(A)). The TEKS standard states: Describe cell theory development and explain that organisms are composed of one or more cells. Students should understand that cells are the basic units of life and that all living organisms are composed of one or more cells. Key concepts: cell theory states that all living things are made of cells, cells are the basic unit of structure and function, and all cells come from pre-existing cells; plant cells have cell walls, chloroplasts, and large vacuoles that animal cells lack; both plant and animal cells have a cell membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, and mitochondria; organisms can be unicellular or multicellular. Vocabulary: cell, cell theory, cell membrane, cell wall, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria, chloroplast, vacuole, organelle, unicellular, multicellular, prokaryotic, eukaryotic. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Investigate how organisms depend on biotic and abiotic factors
Science.prompt: This topic covers photosynthesis for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(A)). The TEKS standard states: Investigate how organisms depend on biotic and abiotic factors. Students should understand that photosynthesis is the process by which plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce food (glucose) and release oxygen. Key concepts: plants are producers because they make their own food; photosynthesis occurs primarily in leaves using chlorophyll (the green pigment); the equation for photosynthesis involves sunlight + water + carbon dioxide producing glucose + oxygen; plants need sunlight, water, carbon dioxide, and nutrients from soil to grow. Students should understand that photosynthesis is the foundation of most food chains because it converts light energy into chemical energy stored in food. Vocabulary: photosynthesis, chlorophyll, producer, glucose, carbon dioxide, oxygen, sunlight, chloroplast, energy, food chain. Problems should involve tracing energy flow and explaining what plants need to make food, appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe hierarchical organization of organism, population, and community
Science.prompt: This topic covers ecosystems and biomes for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(C)). The TEKS standard states: Describe hierarchical organization of organism, population, and community. Students should understand the hierarchical organization of ecological systems from individual organisms to the biosphere. Key concepts: an organism is a single living thing; a population is a group of the same species living in the same area; a community consists of all populations in an area; an ecosystem includes the community plus nonliving factors; biomes are large regions with similar climate and organisms. Students should describe how organisms, populations, and communities interact within ecosystems and understand factors that define different biomes. Vocabulary: organism, population, community, ecosystem, biome, biosphere, habitat, niche, abiotic, biotic, diversity, hierarchy. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe predatory, competitive, and symbiotic relationships
Science.prompt: This topic covers food webs and food chains for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.12(B)). The TEKS standard states: Describe predatory, competitive, and symbiotic relationships. Students should understand that energy flows through ecosystems from producers to consumers to decomposers. Key concepts: producers (plants, algae) make their own food through photosynthesis; primary consumers (herbivores) eat producers; secondary consumers (carnivores) eat primary consumers; tertiary consumers eat secondary consumers; decomposers break down dead organisms and recycle nutrients; food chains show a single path of energy flow; food webs show interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. Students should be able to predict how changes in one population affect others in the food web. Vocabulary: producer, consumer, decomposer, herbivore, carnivore, omnivore, predator, prey, food chain, food web, energy pyramid, ecosystem, trophic level. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Identify and compare characteristics of organisms (prokaryotic/eukaryotic, unicellular/multicellular)
Science.prompt: This topic covers classification of organisms for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(B)). The TEKS standard states: Identify and compare characteristics of organisms (prokaryotic/eukaryotic, unicellular/multicellular). Students should understand that organisms can be classified based on shared characteristics. Key concepts: organisms are classified as prokaryotic (no nucleus, like bacteria) or eukaryotic (have a nucleus); organisms can be unicellular (one cell) or multicellular (many cells); the main kingdoms include bacteria, archaea, protists, fungi, plants, and animals; classification is based on shared structural and functional characteristics. Students should compare and contrast different types of organisms using observable features. Vocabulary: classification, prokaryotic, eukaryotic, unicellular, multicellular, kingdom, domain, bacteria, archaea, protist, fungi, plant, animal, cell, nucleus, taxonomy. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe how variations within populations influence survival
Science.prompt: This topic covers adaptations of organisms for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(C)). The TEKS standard states: Describe how variations within populations influence survival. Students should understand that organisms have structural and behavioral adaptations that help them survive in their specific environments. Key concepts: structural adaptations are physical features (e.g., thick fur for cold climates, webbed feet for swimming); behavioral adaptations are actions organisms take to survive (e.g., migration, hibernation); variations within a population mean some individuals may be better suited to survive changes in the environment; over time, populations change as organisms with advantageous traits survive and reproduce more successfully. Vocabulary: adaptation, structural, behavioral, variation, population, environment, survival, natural selection, camouflage, mimicry, hibernate, migrate. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Identify and compare characteristics of organisms
Science.prompt: This topic covers human body systems for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(B)). The TEKS standard states: Identify and compare characteristics of organisms. Students should understand that the human body is organized into systems that work together to carry out life functions. Key concepts: major body systems include skeletal (support and protection), muscular (movement), digestive (breaking down food), circulatory (transporting blood, oxygen, nutrients), respiratory (gas exchange), nervous (sending signals), and excretory (removing waste). Students should understand how structures within each system are specialized for their function and how systems interact with each other. Vocabulary: organ, tissue, system, function, structure, skeleton, muscle, digestion, circulation, respiration, nerve, cell. Students should connect body system functions to everyday experiences like breathing, eating, and moving. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe how variations within populations influence survival
Science.prompt: This topic covers genetics and heredity for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(C)). The TEKS standard states: Describe how variations within populations influence survival. Students should understand basic concepts of how traits are passed from parents to offspring. Key concepts: heredity is the passing of traits from parents to offspring; traits are characteristics of organisms (e.g., eye color, height, flower color); some traits are inherited (genetic) while others are influenced by the environment; offspring share traits with their parents but also show variation; variations within a population can influence survival. Students should understand that genetic information is carried in cells and that offspring are not identical copies of their parents. Vocabulary: heredity, trait, inherited, gene, offspring, parent, variation, generation, dominant, recessive, DNA, genetic. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe how variations within populations influence survival
Science.prompt: This topic covers natural selection and evolution for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.13(C)). The TEKS standard states: Describe how variations within populations influence survival. Students should understand the basic principles of natural selection as a mechanism for how populations change over time. Key concepts: within any population, there is natural variation in traits; organisms compete for limited resources (food, water, shelter); individuals with traits better suited to their environment are more likely to survive and reproduce; over many generations, advantageous traits become more common in a population. Students should understand that natural selection acts on existing variations and does not involve organisms choosing to change. Vocabulary: natural selection, variation, adaptation, population, environment, survival, reproduction, competition, trait, generation, evolution, species, fitness. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Model and describe Earth's layers (inner core, outer core, mantle, crust)
Science.prompt: This topic covers Earth's layers and plate tectonics for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.10(B)). The TEKS standard states: Model and describe Earth's layers (inner core, outer core, mantle, crust). Students should understand the structure of Earth's interior and how plate tectonics shapes Earth's surface. Key concepts: Earth has four main layers: inner core (solid, mostly iron), outer core (liquid metal), mantle (semi-solid, convection currents), and crust (thin, solid outer layer); the crust is divided into tectonic plates that float on the mantle; plate movements cause earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and mountain formation; plates can converge (collide), diverge (separate), or transform (slide past each other). Vocabulary: inner core, outer core, mantle, crust, tectonic plate, earthquake, volcano, mountain, converge, diverge, transform, fault, magma, convection. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe how metamorphic, igneous, and sedimentary rocks form through geologic processes
Science.prompt: This topic covers rocks and minerals for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.10(C)). The TEKS standard states: Describe how metamorphic, igneous, and sedimentary rocks form through geologic processes. Students should understand that rocks are composed of minerals and are classified by how they form. Key concepts: igneous rocks form from cooled magma or lava; sedimentary rocks form from compressed and cemented layers of sediment; metamorphic rocks form when existing rocks are changed by heat and pressure; the rock cycle describes continuous transformation between rock types; minerals have specific properties including hardness, luster, color, and streak that can be used for identification. Vocabulary: igneous, sedimentary, metamorphic, rock cycle, mineral, magma, lava, sediment, weathering, erosion, deposition, compaction, cementation, crystal, hardness, luster. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe how rocks form and change through geologic processes including weathering
Science.prompt: This topic covers erosion for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.10(C)). The TEKS standard states: Describe how rocks form and change through geologic processes including weathering. Students should understand that erosion is the process by which weathered rock and soil are moved from one location to another by natural forces. Key concepts: agents of erosion include water (rivers, waves, rain), wind, ice (glaciers), and gravity; weathering breaks rocks into smaller pieces while erosion transports those pieces; deposition occurs when eroded material is dropped in a new location. Students should understand slow versus rapid changes to Earth's surface and how human activities can increase or decrease erosion. Vocabulary: erosion, weathering, deposition, sediment, glacier, landslide, delta, canyon, sand dune, prevention, conservation. Problems should include real-world examples and modeling activities appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.skill: The water cycle and weather patterns
Science.teks_description: Differentiate between biosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and geosphere
Science.prompt: This topic covers the water cycle for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.10(A)). The TEKS standard states: Differentiate between biosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and geosphere. Students should understand that water continuously moves through Earth's systems in a cycle driven by the Sun's energy. Key concepts: evaporation (liquid water becomes water vapor due to heat), condensation (water vapor cools and forms clouds), precipitation (water falls as rain, snow, sleet, or hail), collection/runoff (water gathers in oceans, lakes, rivers, and groundwater). Students should understand that the Sun provides the energy that drives the water cycle and that the total amount of water on Earth remains relatively constant. Vocabulary: water cycle, evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, groundwater, collection, water vapor, clouds, transpiration, aquifer. Students should be able to model and describe each stage of the cycle. Problems should connect to real-world weather observations appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Model how tilted Earth revolves around the Sun, causing seasonal changes
Science.prompt: This topic covers climate and climate change for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.9(A)). The TEKS standard states: Model how tilted Earth revolves around the Sun, causing seasonal changes. Students should understand that climate is the long-term pattern of weather in a region, influenced by Earth's tilt and revolution around the Sun. Key concepts: seasons are caused by Earth's tilted axis (23.5 degrees) as it revolves around the Sun, causing different parts of Earth to receive varying amounts of direct sunlight; climate varies by latitude, altitude, and proximity to water; climate zones include tropical, temperate, and polar; factors affecting climate include latitude, ocean currents, wind patterns, and elevation. Vocabulary: climate, weather, season, tilt, axis, revolution, latitude, altitude, temperature, precipitation, tropical, temperate, polar, climate zone. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Research and describe importance of resource management in reducing energy use, poverty, malnutrition, and pollution
Science.prompt: This topic covers renewable and nonrenewable resources for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.11(A)). The TEKS standard states: Research and describe importance of resource management in reducing energy use, poverty, malnutrition, and pollution. Students should understand the difference between renewable and nonrenewable resources and the importance of responsible resource management. Key concepts: renewable resources can be replenished naturally in a relatively short time (solar, wind, water, biomass); nonrenewable resources exist in limited quantities and take millions of years to form (fossil fuels like coal, oil, natural gas; minerals); overuse of resources leads to environmental problems including pollution, habitat destruction, and climate change; conservation, recycling, and using renewable energy sources help reduce environmental impact. Vocabulary: renewable, nonrenewable, resource, fossil fuel, conservation, recycling, solar energy, wind energy, pollution, sustainability, environmental impact. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Identify and explain how forces act on objects (gravity, friction, magnetism, applied, normal)
Science.prompt: This topic covers forces and motion including Newton's Laws for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.7(A)). Students should understand that forces cause changes in the motion of objects and that Newton's three laws describe these relationships. Key concepts: Newton's First Law (an object at rest stays at rest, an object in motion stays in motion unless acted upon by an unbalanced force - inertia); Newton's Second Law (force = mass x acceleration, or F = ma); Newton's Third Law (every action has an equal and opposite reaction); types of forces include gravity, friction, applied force, normal force, and magnetism; net force determines whether forces are balanced or unbalanced. Vocabulary: force, motion, Newton's Laws, inertia, acceleration, mass, friction, gravity, balanced, unbalanced, net force, action, reaction, applied force, normal force, magnetism. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Calculate net force and determine if forces are balanced or unbalanced
Science.prompt: This topic covers simple machines for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.7(B)). The TEKS standard states: Calculate net force and determine if forces are balanced or unbalanced. Students should understand that simple machines make work easier by changing the direction or magnitude of a force. Key concepts: the six simple machines are lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, and screw; each machine provides a mechanical advantage by reducing the force needed or changing the direction of force; net force is the combination of all forces acting on an object; balanced forces result in no change in motion while unbalanced forces cause acceleration. Students should calculate net force and determine if forces are balanced or unbalanced. Vocabulary: simple machine, lever, pulley, wheel and axle, inclined plane, wedge, screw, force, work, mechanical advantage, net force, balanced, unbalanced. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe how energy is conserved through transfers and transformations
Science.prompt: This topic covers energy transformations for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.8(B)). The TEKS standard states: Describe how energy is conserved through transfers and transformations. Students should understand that energy exists in multiple forms and can be transformed from one form to another. Key concepts: forms of energy include kinetic (motion), potential (stored), thermal (heat), light, sound, electrical, and chemical; energy transformations occur constantly (e.g., a lamp transforms electrical energy to light and thermal energy); the law of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed or transferred. Students should trace energy transformations in everyday devices and natural processes. Vocabulary: energy, kinetic energy, potential energy, thermal energy, electrical energy, chemical energy, light energy, sound energy, transformation, transfer, conservation. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Explain how energy transfers through transverse and longitudinal waves
Science.prompt: This topic covers waves including sound and light for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.8(C)). The TEKS standard states: Explain how energy transfers through transverse and longitudinal waves. Students should understand that energy can be transferred through transverse and longitudinal waves. Key concepts: transverse waves have particles that move perpendicular to the wave direction (e.g., light, water surface waves); longitudinal waves have particles that move parallel to the wave direction (e.g., sound); wave properties include wavelength, frequency, and amplitude; sound requires a medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to travel while light can travel through a vacuum; energy is conserved as it transfers through waves. Vocabulary: transverse wave, longitudinal wave, wavelength, frequency, amplitude, crest, trough, compression, rarefaction, medium, vibration, energy transfer. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.skill: The scientific method and experimental design
Science.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct descriptive, comparative, and experimental investigations
Science.prompt: This topic covers scientific investigations and the scientific method for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.1(B)). The TEKS standard states: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct descriptive, comparative, and experimental investigations. Students should understand that scientific investigations follow systematic processes to explore questions about the natural world. Key concepts: asking testable questions, forming hypotheses, designing fair tests with controlled variables, collecting and recording data, analyzing results, and drawing evidence-based conclusions. Students should understand the difference between independent variables (what you change), dependent variables (what you measure), and controlled variables (what you keep the same). They should also understand the importance of repeating trials for reliability. Vocabulary: hypothesis, variable, independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, data, evidence, conclusion, experiment, observation, prediction, fair test, trial, reliability. Problems should involve designing experiments and identifying variables, appropriate for 11-12 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Evaluate experimental and engineering designs
Science.prompt: This topic covers the engineering design process for Grade 6 students (TEKS 6.2(D)). The TEKS standard states: Evaluate experimental and engineering designs. Students should understand that the engineering design process is a systematic approach to solving problems and creating solutions. Key concepts: the process includes identifying a problem, researching solutions, designing and planning, building a prototype, testing and evaluating, and improving the design; engineers use criteria (requirements the solution must meet) and constraints (limitations like time, cost, and materials); designs should be evaluated based on how well they meet the criteria and work within constraints; the process is iterative, meaning designs are continuously improved based on testing results. Vocabulary: engineering, design process, criteria, constraint, prototype, evaluate, iterate, improve, solution, problem, test, data. Problems should be appropriate for 11-12 year olds.
